items.q: In a tropical rainforest nutrient cycle, where is most of the nutrient stored?
items.correct: In the biomass (living plants). Litter is rapidly decomposed by warm humid conditions, and soil is leached — so cycling between biomass and litter dominates.
items.misconception: Most nutrients in a rainforest are stored in the soil — that's why plants grow so well.
items.random_wrong: Most rainforest nutrients are stored in animals.
items.why: Rainforest soils are notoriously thin and infertile. The Gersmehl model shows biomass as the dominant store — which is why slash-and-burn agriculture collapses quickly when biomass is removed.

items.q: What is a positive climate feedback loop?
items.correct: A process where warming triggers changes that cause more warming — e.g. melting Arctic ice exposes dark ocean, which absorbs more heat, melting more ice (ice-albedo feedback).
items.misconception: Positive feedback means a good outcome for the climate.
items.random_wrong: Positive feedback means the climate balances itself out.
items.why: 'Positive' here means amplifying, not beneficial. Negative feedbacks (which stabilise) are positive for us. The terminology trips students up — it refers to direction of change, not desirability.

items.q: Why does correlation not imply causation in geographical data?
items.correct: Two variables may correlate because of a third confounding factor, coincidence, or reverse causation — statistical association alone does not prove one causes the other.
items.misconception: If two variables correlate strongly, one must cause the other.
items.random_wrong: Correlation and causation are the same thing.
items.why: Classic example: ice cream sales correlate with drownings — both caused by hot weather, neither causes the other. In geography this matters for evaluating claims like 'urbanisation causes obesity'.
It's Better jobs in cities. “Drought in countryside” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: A poor, overcrowded city area. “A rich district” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Roughly what percentage of people now live in urban areas (global)?
items.why: The real figure is 55. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
